3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
157	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
159	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
161	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
163	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
164	  % topological spaces."
165	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
166	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
167	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
168	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
169	\end{lemma}
170	
171	\begin{proof}
172	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
173	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
174	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
175	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
176	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
177	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
178	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
179	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
180	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
181	  flasque.
182	\end{proof}
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	[Cokernel of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence is flasque]
186	  \label{lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact}
187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque}
188	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
189	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
190	  % Hartshorne~II.1~Exercise~1.16(c) / III.2.5 chain (iter-193 Lane H
191	  % prover-side construction). The classical reference is Hartshorne
192	  % II.1, Exercise~1.16(c); see the verbatim quote in the proof of
193	  % \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} below.
194	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate (Hartshorne~II.1,
195	  Exercise~1.16(c) packaged with the sections-surjectivity input from
196	  II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) as a hypothesis).}
197	  Let $X$ be a topological space and let
198	  \[
199	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
200	  \]
201	  be a short exact sequence in
202	  $\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
203	  (\Module \bar k)$. Suppose:
204	  \begin{enumerate}
205	    \item $\mathcal F_2$ is flasque (in the sense of
206	      \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}); and
207	    \item the section-level map
208	      $\mathcal F_2(U) \to \mathcal F_3(U)$ is surjective for every open
209	      $U \subseteq X$ (the Hartshorne~II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) input,
210	      packaged as a hypothesis rather than invoked internally — this
211	      keeps the lemma's axiom-set clean and lets the consumer site
212	      supply the surjectivity from its own substrate).
213	  \end{enumerate}
214	  Then the cokernel sheaf $\mathcal F_3$ is flasque.
215	
216	  Concretely, given $V \le U$ in $\Opens X$ and a section
217	  $t \in \mathcal F_3(V)$: lift $t$ to $\tilde t \in \mathcal F_2(V)$
218	  via hypothesis~(2) at $V$, extend $\tilde t$ to
219	  $\tilde T \in \mathcal F_2(U)$ via flasqueness of $\mathcal F_2$, and
220	  push $\tilde T$ through the map $\mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3$ at
221	  $U$. Naturality of the morphism on opens identifies the restriction
222	  of the resulting section in $\mathcal F_3(U)$ with the original $t$.
223	\end{lemma}
224	
225	\begin{lemma}
226	[$\Ext^{n+1}$ vanishes from $\Ext^n$ vanishing on the rightmost term, with injective middle term]
227	  \label{lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
228	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero}
229	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
230	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
231	  % Hartshorne~III.2.5 induction (iter-193 Lane H prover-side
232	  % construction). The underlying mathematical statement is the
233	  % classical degree-shifting argument that, for an injective middle
234	  % term in a short exact sequence, the connecting morphism in the
235	  % long exact $\Ext$ sequence is surjective above the injective
236	  % dimension. No external source is cited verbatim.
237	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate.}
238	  Let $\mathcal C$ be an abelian category equipped with a
239	  \texttt{HasExt} instance, let $X$ be an object of $\mathcal C$, and
240	  let
241	  \[
242	    0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0
243	  \]
244	  be a short exact sequence in $\mathcal C$ whose middle term $S_2$ is
245	  injective. Let $n_0 \ge 1$ be a natural number and assume that
246	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ (i.e.\ every element of $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3)$
247	  vanishes). Then $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1) = 0$.
248	
249	  The proof is the classical degree-shift. Since $S_2$ is injective and
250	  $n_0 + 1 \ge 2$, the injective-dimension subsingleton instance forces
251	  $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_2)$ to be subsingleton; combined with exactness
252	  of the covariant $\Ext$ long exact sequence at $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X,
253	  S_1)$, every $x_1 \in \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$ is in the image of the
254	  connecting morphism $\delta \colon \Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
255	  \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$. The hypothesis
256	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ then gives $x_1 = 0$. The lemma is consumed
257	  in the $i \ge 2$ inductive case of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{theorem}
261	
262	\leanok
263	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
264	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
266	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
267	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
268	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
269	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
270	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
271	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
272	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
273	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
274	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
275	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
276	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
277	  \[
278	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
279	  \]
280	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
281	\end{theorem}
282	
283	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
284	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
285	% be the quotient:
286	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
287	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
288	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
289	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
290	% Ex. 1.16b)
291	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
292	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
293	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
294	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
295	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
296	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
297	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
298	% \(i\) we get the result."
299	\begin{proof}
300	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
301	  \leanok
302	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
303	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
304	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
305	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
306	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
307	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
308	  short exact sequence
309	  \[
310	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
311	  \]
312	  in the sheaf category.
313	
314	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
315	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
316	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
317	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
318	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
319	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
320	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
321	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
322	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
323	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
324	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
325	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
326	
327	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
328	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
329	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
330	  exact sequence on global sections
331	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
332	  \[
333	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
334	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
335	  \]
336	
337	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
338	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
339	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
340	  positive degree:
341	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
342	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
343	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
344	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
345	  \[
346	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
347	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
348	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
349	  \]
350	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
351	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
352	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
353	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
354	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
355	
356	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
357	  exact sequence yields
358	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
359	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
360	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
361	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
362	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
363	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
364	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
365	
366	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
367	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
368	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
369	  % already provided by chapter
370	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
371	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
372	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
373	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
374	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
375	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
376	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
377	  % LOC apiece.
378	\end{proof}

380	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
381	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
382	
383	\begin{lemma}
384	
385	\leanok
386	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
387	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
389	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
390	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
391	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
392	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
393	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
394	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
395	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
396	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
397	  % X\) is the inclusion."
398	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
399	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
400	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
401	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
402	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
403	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
404	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
405	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
406	\end{lemma}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
410	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
411	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
412	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
413	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
414	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
415	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
416	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
417	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
418	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
419	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
420	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
421	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
422	\end{proof}

424	\begin{lemma}
425	
426	\leanok
427	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
428	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
430	
431	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
432	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
433	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
434	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
435	
436	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
437	  no external source pinned).
438	\end{lemma}
439	
440	\begin{proof}
441	
442	\leanok
443	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
444	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
445	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
446	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
447	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
448	\end{proof}

452	\leanok

471	  above).
472	\end{lemma}

474	\begin{proof}
475	  \leanok
476	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward, lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible, lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
477	  By
478	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
479	  isomorphic to the pushforward
480	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
481	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88

99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be

107	  % \varphi$."

111	  \[

113	  \]

115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[

118	  \]

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188

210	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]

213	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
214	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
215	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
216	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
217	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
218	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
219	  \(S_M\)-module.

224	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
225	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
226	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's

230	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's

433	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
434	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
435	
436	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
437	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
438	point.
439	
440	\begin{theorem}
441

443	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
444	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}

446	  
447	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
448	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
450	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
451	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
452	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
453	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
454	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
455	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
456	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
457	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
458	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
459	
460	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
461	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
462	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
463	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
464	  in \(X\).

542	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)

544	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
545	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.

548	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of

550	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of

556	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
557	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
558	
559	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
560	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
561	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
562	this forces the locus to be empty.
563

577	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
578	  % divisors).}"

581	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow

583	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
584	  union of prime divisors).

587	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
588	\end{lemma}

627	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
628	
629	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
630	  \[
631	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
632	    \qquad

664	  \(K(X \times X)\):

669	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
670	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
671	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).

674	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor

676	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function

678	  \[
679	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
680	  \]
681	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
682	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
683	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
684	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with

686	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
687	  \[
688	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
689	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
690	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
691	  \]

693	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
694	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
695	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
696	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)

698	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension

700	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).

703	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in

800	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
801	      \]
802	  \end{enumerate}
803	
804	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
805	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
806	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
807	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
808	\end{theorem}
809	
810	\begin{proof}
811	  
812	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
813	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
814	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
815	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
816	  \[
817	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
818	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
819	  \]
820	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
821	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
822	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
823	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
824	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
825	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
826	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
827	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
828	
829	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
830	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
831	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
832	\end{proof}
833

835	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}

837	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
838	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in

849

874	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands

1008	\section{Out of scope}
1009	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1010	
1011	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1012	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1013	scope:
1014	\begin{itemize}
1015	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1016	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1017	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1018	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1019	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1020	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1021	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1022	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1023	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1024	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1025	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1026	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1027	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1028	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1029	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1030	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1031	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1032	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1033	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1034	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1035	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1036	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1037	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1038	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1039	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1040	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1041	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1042	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1043	    introduced.
1044	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1045	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1046	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1047	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1048	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1049	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1050	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
1051	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1052	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1053	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1054	\end{itemize}

102	    Function.Surjective ((F.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom)

299	**Proof sketch** (Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b), Zorn's lemma):
300	Let `t ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`. Consider pairs `(V, s)` where `V ⊆ U` is
301	open and `s ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op V)` restricts to `t|_V`. Order by
302	extension. Any chain has an upper bound (sheaf gluing condition on
303	`S.X₂`). By Zorn's lemma, a maximal element `(V₀, s₀)` exists. Suppose
304	`V₀ ≠ U`. Pick `x ∈ U \ V₀`. Stalkwise, `S.X₂_x → S.X₃_x` is surjective
305	(SES in the abelian sheaf category preserves stalks). So in some

310	`α ∈ S.X₁.val.obj (op W)`. Set `s'' := s'_W + α`. Then `s''` agrees with
311	`s₀` on `V₀ ∩ W`. By sheaf gluing on `S.X₂`, get a unique
312	`s''' ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op (V₀ ∪ W))` extending both. This contradicts
313	maximality. Hence `V₀ = U`.

69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	* `indeterminacyLocus f : Set X` returns the carrier of the closed
75	  complement of `f.domain` — not `∅` or `Set.univ`.
76	* `CodimOneFree f : Prop` is the genuine ∀-statement over codim-1 points,
77	  not `True`.
78	* `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one` produces a Mathlib `IsDiscreteValuationRing`
79	  instance — not a trivial proposition.
80	* `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` asserts `∃! g, g.toRationalMap = f`
81	  — the existence of a unique regular extension.
82	* `indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme` asserts a disjunction:
83	  either the locus is empty or every component has coheight `1`.
84	* `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` asserts the (truthful)
85	  iff between definedness at `W.point` and the existence of a `PartialMap`
86	  representative containing `W.point` in its domain — a definitional unfold
87	  of `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` with the
88	  conjunction swapped for downstream ergonomics.
89	
90	Unfolding any pinned declaration exposes the named substantive content
91	(the carrier set complement, the `∀ η, ...` statement, the DVR instance,
92	the unique regular extension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / empty-content
93	`proof_wanted` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` placeholders are used.
94	
95	## Variety conventions
96	
97	Following the project (cf. `Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean`,
98	`AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`), a **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** is an
99	object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:
100	
101	* `[Smooth X.hom]`, `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
102	* `[IsSeparated X.hom]`, `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
103	* `[IsIntegral X.left]`, `[IsReduced X.left]`.
104	
105	A **complete variety** adds `[IsProper Y.hom]`. A **group variety** over
106	`k̄` adds a `[GrpObj Y]` instance to the variety package.

127	/-! ## §1. The indeterminacy locus of a rational map
128	
129	The indeterminacy locus `Z(f) ⊆ X` of a rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is the closed
130	complement of its domain of definition `Dom(f) := f.domain` (a Mathlib-shipped
131	open subset of `X`).
132	
133	Blueprint pin: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne §I.3 p. 16). -/
134	
135	/-- **Indeterminacy locus of a rational map.** The closed complement of the
136	domain of definition `f.domain` (an `X.Opens`, exposed by Mathlib at
137	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap`):
138	
139	`Z(f) := X ∖ Dom(f) ⊆ X`.
140	
141	`f` is everywhere defined (i.e. extends to a regular morphism) iff
142	`indeterminacyLocus f = ∅`.
143	
144	Blueprint reference: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
145	§I.3, p. 16). -/
146	def indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Set X :=
147	  (f.domain : Set X)ᶜ
148	
149	/-- The indeterminacy locus of a rational map is closed (it is the complement
150	of an open). -/
151	lemma isClosed_indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) :
152	    IsClosed (indeterminacyLocus f) := by
153	  unfold indeterminacyLocus
154	  exact (f.domain.isOpen).isClosed_compl
155	
156	/-! ## §2. Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps
157	
158	A rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is **codim-1-indeterminacy-free** if its
159	indeterminacy locus contains no prime divisor of `X`, i.e. every codim-1
160	generic point of `X` already lies in `f.domain`. Equivalently (under the
161	project's standing `Order.coheight` convention on the specialisation
162	preorder of `X.carrier`), every `η : X` with `Order.coheight η = 1` lies in
163	`f.domain`.
164	
165	Blueprint pin: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1, the
166	conclusion). -/
167	
168	/-- **Codim-1-indeterminacy-free rational map.** A rational map
169	`f : X ⇢ Y` is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if every codimension-one point
170	of `X` lies in its domain of definition. Equivalently: no prime divisor of
171	`X` (i.e. closure of a codim-1 generic point) is contained in
172	`indeterminacyLocus f`.
173	
174	The codim-1 condition is encoded via `Order.coheight η = 1` on the
175	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier`, matching the project's standing
176	convention in `Scheme.PrimeDivisor` (`RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`).
177	
178	Blueprint reference: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
179	Varieties*, Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
180	def CodimOneFree {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Prop :=
181	  ∀ (x : X), Order.coheight x = 1 → x ∈ f.domain
182	
183	end RationalMap

185	/-! ## §3. Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point
186	
187	On a nonsingular variety `X` over `k̄`, the local ring at the generic point
188	of a prime divisor is a discrete valuation ring with fraction field equal to
189	the function field `K(X) = X.functionField`. This is Hartshorne II.6's
190	"regular in codimension one" property combined with the regular-local-ring-of-dim-1
191	$\Leftrightarrow$ DVR equivalence (Atiyah–Macdonald 9.2; Stacks `00PD`).
192	
193	Blueprint pin: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130). -/

638	
639	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a complete variety has
640	indeterminacy locus of codimension `≥ 2`. Specialising further: if `f` is
641	already codim-1-indeterminacy-free (by hypothesis), then `Dom(f) = X` and
642	`f` extends to a unique regular morphism `X ⟶ Y`.

647	source + complete target ⇒ extension.**

650	`Y` a complete variety over `k̄`, and `f : X ⇢ Y` a codim-1-indeterminacy-free
651	rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism `g : X ⟶ Y`
652	whose induced rational map equals `f`.

654	The proof combines two steps:
655	* **Step 1** (codim-1 ruling-out): the indeterminacy locus has no codim-1

657	  `W ⊆ Z(f)` the DVR property of the stalk (`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`)

664	  enough on the dim-≤2 cases the project consumes; Step 2 is what extends
